### Device findings that matter commercially

1. **Elo Pay M100 is one of the clearest bridge products** between the payment-first and SaaS-first camps. It is a **10.1-inch Android 12 tablet** with **integrated EMV/NFC**, optional **SE4100 1D/2D barcode scanner**, optional LTE, user-replaceable **8000mAh battery**, and a **rotating dock** that converts the device from mobile to fixed POS. That combination is unusual because most integrated-payment devices are smaller handhelds, while most 10-inch tablets require external readers.

2. **Square and Shopify still embody the modular tablet model**. Square Stand and Kiosk remain iPad-dependent, and Shopify’s POS Terminal/Countertop Kit is explicitly a paired setup rather than a single integrated slab. This lowers initial hardware specialization but increases dependency on tablet compatibility, OS support, and the peripheral bill of materials.

3. **PAX, Ingenico, and Verifone increasingly compete on app-capable payment hardware rather than just certification**. PAX emphasizes Android SmartPOS and app distribution through MAXSTORE/PAXSTORE; Ingenico’s AXIUM family adds broader screen formats and accessory options; Verifone’s Victa Mobile pushes further toward phone-like form factors with a **6.7-inch screen** and **4GB/32GB** compute profile.

4. **Rugged enterprise tablets are usually selected for workflow intensity, not payments**. Zebra, Honeywell, and Samsung add value through scan engines, docks, hot-swap batteries, enterprise device management, and longer support windows. Payment support exists, but it is rarely the primary selection criterion.

---

## Use-Case Analysis: Payments vs SaaS Workflow Deployments

### Payment-focused vs SaaS-focused device comparison table

| Dimension | Payment-focused tablet-style devices | SaaS-focused tablet deployments |
|---|---|---|
| Typical hardware | Integrated EMV/NFC, often printer/camera/scanner | Larger screen, ruggedness, docks, scanners, external reader options |
| Typical buyer | Acquirer, PSP, or SMB choosing turnkey payments | Retail/restaurant operator, enterprise IT, field ops, omnichannel retailer |
| Peripheral attach rate | Lower in simple setups; higher in counter bundles | Higher: scanners, printers, docks, keyboards, vehicle mounts |
| Software stack | Certified payment kernel + lightweight business apps | Full POS/SaaS workflow + modular payments |
| Replacement cycle | Often driven by certification, security, processor programs | Driven by OS lifecycle, ruggedness, breakage, workflow change |
| Economics | Hardware often subsidized or bundled to pull processing volume | Hardware often merchant-owned; software and peripherals are larger share of TCO |
| Strengths | Faster deployment, compliance, lower setup complexity | Flexibility, broader workflow support, easier vertical customization |
| Weaknesses | More lock-in, less hardware openness | More components, more integration points |

### Analysis

The core trade-off is **certified-payment simplicity versus workflow flexibility**. Worldline explicitly describes SmartPOS as a way to load business applications directly onto an Android payment terminal, turning the device into a multi-purpose merchant tool while retaining payment acceptance. PAX and Ingenico make the same move: the device begins as a secure payment endpoint, then grows upward into loyalty, order-taking, stock, or service workflows.

The causal mechanism is important. Once a payment terminal can run apps, the merchant avoids buying and managing a separate tablet for basic operational tasks. That reduces device count and onboarding time, which is especially valuable in bank- or acquirer-led deployments. But the merchant also accepts tighter coupling to the processor’s hardware approvals, app distribution model, and lifecycle timetable.

In the **SaaS-first** model—Square Stand, Shopify POS on iPad/Android, Zebra ET-series, Honeywell tablets, Samsung Tab Active—the device is the **primary business computer**. Payment becomes one module among many. That architecture wins when the workflow is richer than checkout alone: tableside ordering, back-of-house inventory, healthcare intake, proof-of-delivery, appointment scheduling, or field service documentation. The price of that flexibility is higher integration burden: more peripherals, more OS compatibility issues, and more responsibility for device management outside the payment processor’s standard stack.

---

## Deployment Scenarios by Vertical

### Use-case mapping table

| Device / family | Retail POS | Restaurant | Line-busting | Self-order / kiosk | Field service | Healthcare | Logistics | Appointment-led SMB |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Clover Flex / Mini / Station / Kiosk | High | High | High | High | Low | Medium | Low | High |
| Square Handheld / Register / Stand / Kiosk | High | High | High | High | Low | Low | Low | High |
| Toast Go / Flex / Kiosk / KDS | Medium | **Very high** | High | High | Low | Low | Low | Medium |
| Shopify tablet + POS Terminal / Hub | High | Medium | Medium | Medium | Low | Low | Low | High |
| PAX A920Pro / A920MAX / SmartTablet | High | High | High | Medium | Medium | Low | Medium | Medium |
| Ingenico DX / RX / CX | High | Medium | High | High | Medium | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| Verifone Victa / V400m / P630 | High | Medium | High | High | Medium | Low | Medium | Medium |
| Elo Pay M100 / EloPOS | High | Medium | Medium | High | Low | Medium | Low | Medium |
| Zebra ET40/45 / ET6x | Medium | Medium | High | Medium | High | High | High | Low |
| Honeywell EDA10A / RT10A | Medium | Low | Medium | Low | High | High | High | Low |
| Samsung Tab Active5 Pro | Medium | Medium | Medium | High | High | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| SUNMI V3 / D3 Pro / T2s | High | High | Medium | Medium | Low | Low | Medium | Medium |

### Deployment mechanisms by vertical

**Retail POS.** Retail checkouts value larger screens, customer-facing displays, inventory sync, barcode support, and accessory attachment. That is why Square Register, Clover Mini/Station, Shopify tablet setups, Elo Pay M100, and SUNMI desktop-class systems are structurally strong in this use case. The hardware job is not only payment capture; it is SKU lookup, staff workflow, and customer confirmation.

**Restaurants.** Restaurant operators overweight handheld mobility, order-entry ergonomics, modifier handling, and kitchen synchronization. This is why **Toast Go**, **Clover Flex**, and **Square Handheld** matter more than generic rugged tablets in tableside deployments. Toast’s explicit **offline continuity** matters here because restaurant service cannot stop when connectivity dips.

**Line-busting and mobile checkout.** Handheld payment terminals and compact tablets win because they collapse queue reduction, order entry, and payment into one operator motion. Square Handheld, Clover Flex, PAX A920-class devices, and Verifone Victa Mobile fit this pattern. The mechanism is labor efficiency: fewer handoffs and less return to a fixed counter.

**Self-service and kiosk.** Larger displays, stable mounts, and customer-facing UI matter more than pure portability. Clover Kiosk, Square Kiosk, Toast Kiosk, Ingenico RX-class, and SUNMI/Elo desktop systems are the most relevant comparison set.

**Field service, logistics, and healthcare.** Zebra, Honeywell, and Samsung dominate because these environments punish fragile hardware. Scan engines, vehicle docks, hand straps, hot-swappable batteries, and long support windows affect uptime more than integrated EMV does. Payment may still be added, but only when it does not compromise field workflow.

---

## Regional Market Analysis: North America

### Market penetration

North America is the **best-documented region** for this category because the leading platforms disclose meaningful ecosystem scale. **Clover says 4M+ devices shipped** and **$337B+ annualized processing volume**. **Square says more than 4 million sellers run on Square**, it processed **$209.6B in payments in 2023**, and it serves **8 countries**. **Toast ended 2025 at approximately 164,000 locations**. These figures are not additive—seller counts, shipped devices, and locations measure different things—but together they establish a much denser installed base than in the other three regions.

Market summaries also consistently place North America as the largest regional revenue block in mobile POS/tablet POS, with shares in the **high-30% range** across summaries. That matters less as a unit count than as confirmation that hardware spending, software attachment, and replacement activity are concentrated here.

### Common usage scenarios

North America’s mix is driven by **software-led merchant operating platforms** rather than by bank-distributed generic terminals. The most common patterns are:
- **Retail countertop POS**: Square Register/Stand, Clover Mini/Station, Shopify tablet + terminal
- **Restaurant handheld + kitchen stack**: Toast Go/Flex/KDS, Clover Flex, Square Handheld
- **Self-service kiosk**: Clover Kiosk, Square Kiosk, Toast Kiosk
- **Omnichannel retail**: Shopify and Square’s tablet-led deployments are strongest here because software, online/offline sync, and consumer tablet familiarity reduce adoption friction### Typical price ranges

Observed North American list or channel prices:
- **Square Reader**: **$59**
- **Square Stand**: **$149**
- **Square Terminal**: **$299**
- **Square Handheld**: **$399**
- **Shopify POS Terminal**: **$349**
- **Shopify POS Terminal Countertop Kit**: **$459**
- **Square Register (2nd gen)**: **$899** on the live hardware page; an older Square-owned explainer cited **$799**, showing that vendor-owned pricing references can drift over time
- **Clover Flex / Mini / Station Solo / Station Duo**: approximately **$749 / $949 / $1,799 / $2,099** in reseller/channel evidence, which is useful directionally but is not official MSRP
- **Toast Starter Kit**: can be offered at **$0 upfront** under processing-tied plans; third-party bundle references place broader restaurant hardware bundles in the several-hundred to low-thousand-dollar range

The mechanism behind visible North American pricing is that providers can expose hardware price separately because they expect recurring monetization from payments and software afterward. Toast’s **$0 upfront** option makes that explicit: the economic decision is not hardware-only capex, but lifetime payment and SaaS revenue.

### Estimated installed base

**Estimate (active tablet-style devices, North America):**
- **Low:** 6.5 million
- **Base:** 9.0 million
- **High:** 12.5 million
- **Confidence:** **Medium**

**Why this range is defensible.** Canada alone had **1,095,251 employer businesses** as of December 2023, with more than three in four having **1–9 employees**—exactly the segment that disproportionately adopts lower-capex tablet POS and mobile checkout tools. The U.S. Census materials confirm the establishment-based framework needed for a larger merchant base, though the accessible excerpt here does not provide the national count. Against that backdrop, the platform indicators—**Clover 4M+ devices shipped**, **Square 4M+ sellers**, **Toast ~164k locations**—make a 9.0 million active device base plausible without forcing aggressive assumptions. The estimate excludes pure smartphone-only acceptance but includes countertop tablet POS, handheld SmartPOS, and clearly deployed business tablets used in checkout-adjacent SaaS workflows.

---

## Regional Market Analysis: Japan and Korea

### Japan

#### Market penetration

Japan’s strongest anchor is policy-linked cashless adoption. METI says the **cashless payment ratio reached 42.8% in 2024**, surpassing the national **40% target**, with **141.0 trillion yen** in cashless payments, including **116.9 trillion yen on credit cards**. KOMOJU reported a **39.3%** cashless ratio in 2023, up from **36.0%** in 2022, and **75.8 million** QR/barcode users in 2023. Another market summary says **merchant acceptance of digital wallets exceeds 3 million terminals nationwide**. That 3 million is not a tablet-style device count, but it is a useful upper-bound ecosystem signal.

#### Common usage scenarios

Japan’s device mix reflects local rail complexity:
- **QR- and wallet-heavy SMB acceptance**, especially where merchants avoid large upfront terminal capex
- **Tourism and transit-linked retail**, where contactless and multilingual wallet acceptance matter
- **Healthcare/admin flows**, where identity and payment can converge on one screen
- **Portable merchant scenarios** such as food trucks and temporary retail, evidenced by PayCAS Mobile case materials

#### Leading devices and channels

The clearest current bundled example is **PayCAS Mobile**, a SoftBank-linked solution built on **PAX A920 / A920MAX-class hardware**, with **Android 10**, a **6.5-inch screen**, integrated printer, **FeliCa support**, and app distribution via **PayCAS DX Store**. PAX separately says Japanese **A920MAX sales nearly doubled year-on-year** as banks and PSPs accelerated deployment. The causal point is that Japan is not adopting tablet-style payment hardware mainly through open device retail; it is adopting it through **service bundles aligned to local payment rails**.

#### Typical price ranges

Public local device pricing is weak in the captured evidence, so the most defensible view is:
- **Observed local device MSRP:** not captured publicly for PayCAS Mobile
- **Global hardware proxies:** PAX A920-class around **$375–$393** from non-Japan resellers
- **Open-market Android smart POS proxy:** SUNMI V3 Plus roughly **$449–$479**

That does **not** mean Japanese merchants actually pay those prices directly. It means public retail pricing is not the main route to market; acquiring and service bundles are.

### Korea

#### Market penetration

South Korea combines **advanced digital-payment behavior** with a still-incomplete hardware refresh. One current summary says the country has **2.9 million merchant locations**, and **Zero Pay is accepted at more than 2 million businesses**. Yet the same source says only **about 10% of stores had compliant NFC readers by March 2025**, and a basic upgrade cost about **KRW 200,000 (~USD 138)**. This is one of the clearest examples in the report where demand-side readiness and supply-side estate modernization diverge.

The mechanism is channel structure. Korea remains heavily **VAN/dealer-led**, with provider-controlled registration, regional dealer assignment, site visits, and installation processes. That slows the speed at which a technically desirable terminal refresh becomes a visible open-market hardware opportunity.

#### Common usage scenarios

Korean deployments cluster around:
- **Chain retail smart checkout**
- **Convenience and department-store omnichannel**
- **Last-mile delivery / curbside / in-aisle portable devices**
- **Duty-free and hospitality**
- **Healthcare authentication/payment workflows**

#### Typical price ranges

Captured public pricing is thin. The strongest visible anchor is:
- **Basic NFC-capable terminal upgrade cost:** about **KRW 200,000 (~USD 138)**

That is not a full smart tablet POS price; it is best read as a **baseline modernization cost**. Higher-end Android smart terminals appear to move mainly through dealer/VAN bundles rather than public e-commerce storefronts.

### Japan + Korea installed-base estimate

**Estimate (active tablet-style devices, Japan + Korea):**
- **Low:** 1.8 million
- **Base:** 2.8 million
- **High:** 4.0 million
- **Confidence:** **Medium-low**

**Why this range is defensible.** Japan’s **3 million+ wallet-accepting terminals** provide a clear upper-bound ecosystem indicator, but many are not tablet-style devices. Korea’s **2.9 million merchant locations** establish a large addressable market, but the **~10% NFC-compliant-reader** figure shows how incomplete the modern hardware estate still is. The base estimate assumes meaningful smart-terminal and tablet deployment in urban retail, hospitality, transport, and services, while discounting heavily for QR-only acceptance, legacy readers, and non-tablet hardware.

---

## Regional Market Analysis: Southeast Asia

### Market penetration

Southeast Asia is the most **hardware-growth-oriented** region in this study, but not the most hardware-dependent. A current market summary values the regional POS terminal market at **USD 4.96 billion in 2025**, rising to **USD 11.8 billion by 2031**, a **15.55% CAGR**. The same source says **contactless accounted for 56.97% of revenue in 2025** and **mobile/portable readers 46.25%**. IDC/2C2P add that digital payments already account for **87% of e-commerce payments in 2023**, rising to **94% by 2028** across six major markets.

Those figures show strong payment digitization, but they do not translate one-for-one into tablet hardware counts. In Southeast Asia, merchants can digitize payments through QR and wallets without buying an expensive certified card terminal. That weakens the historical link between “more cashless payments” and “more dedicated POS tablets”.

### Common usage scenarios

The region’s dominant use cases differ from North America:
- **SME and micro-merchant cash-register replacement** using low-cost Android tablets or smart terminals
- **Kiosks, pop-ups, food trucks, and mobile merchants**
- **QR-led and wallet-led acceptance**
- **Omnichannel growth among larger merchants**

A useful scale signal is that **DealPOS and Qasir together serve 500,000 merchants in Indonesia**. That does not mean 500,000 tablet endpoints, but it does show that SaaS-led merchant operating stacks have reached material scale in the largest Southeast Asian market.

### Why adoption looks different here

The most important causal difference is payment rail mix. IDC/2C2P show very large local-wallet and domestic-payment user bases, including **125.1 million mobile-wallet users in Indonesia**, **46.2 million in the Philippines**, and **68.5 million PromptPay users in Thailand**. For many merchants, the first digital acceptance upgrade is therefore **QR/wallet capability**, not a full card-centric POS replacement. That favors:
- lower-cost Android endpoints,
- shared merchant devices,
- tablet-plus-QR workflows,
- and software-led SMB tools that can run on general tablets or low-cost smart terminals.

### Typical price ranges

The price structure is bifurcated:
- **Open-market Android handheld SmartPOS proxies**: PAX A920-class at roughly **$375–$455**, SUNMI V3 family around **$439–$479**
- **Broader “modern contactless-enabled terminal” / fuller-solution figures**: **$1,500–$3,000 upfront plus $500–$1,000 yearly fees** in one regional summary

These are not contradictory; they refer to different commercial packages. The lower figures resemble **device-only handheld SmartPOS**. The higher figures likely reflect **full business systems or enterprise bundles** including software, service, and installation.

### Estimated installed base

**Estimate (active tablet-style devices, Southeast Asia):**
- **Low:** 2.2 million
- **Base:** 3.8 million
- **High:** 5.8 million
- **Confidence:** **Low-medium**

**Why this range is defensible.** The market clearly has strong SME digitization and very high growth, but physical-device counting is difficult because many merchants accept QR/wallet payments without deploying a high-end tablet POS. The base case assumes millions of active Android smartPOS and tablet endpoints region-wide, with **Indonesia the largest contributor**, but discounts heavily for QR-only and software-only acceptance patterns.

---

## Regional Market Analysis: South America

### Market penetration

South America’s most important current story is **Brazil**, where smart terminals have become a core merchant-acquisition tool. Brazil’s POS terminal market is estimated at **USD 6.45 billion in 2025**, rising to **USD 9.16 billion by 2031**. **Mobile and portable solutions accounted for 53.97% of Brazil’s POS terminal market size in 2025** and are projected to post the fastest CAGR through 2031. A broader South America summary says **mobile/portable devices accounted for 63.72% of regional shipments in 2024** and that Brazil represented **34.72% of South American POS terminal revenue in 2024**.

Brazil also has unusually strong contactless signals. IHL says the country has **68% contactless terminal penetration across the installed POS base**, while Mordor says **contactless card payments reached 67.2% of in-person card transactions by December 2024**. Those are different measures, but directionally they tell the same story: merchants and consumers are both operating in a highly contactless environment.

### Common usage scenarios

Brazilian and regional use cases extend beyond fixed retail counters:
- **Micro-merchants and SMB checkout**
- **Restaurants and tableside service**
- **Transportation and ticketing**
- **Logistics and proof-of-delivery**
- **Healthcare front desk and kiosk**
- **Professional services and mobile billing**

This breadth follows from the hardware design. Android smart terminals now function as compact operating endpoints, not just card readers. That matters especially in Brazil because merchants often want instant settlement, lightweight business analytics, and mobility in one device.

### Why Brazil is different

The crucial mechanism is **fintech-acquirer subsidy economics**. Mordor says acquirers are shifting capital toward Android smart terminals that can converge EMV contactless and **Pix NFC payloads**, and are **subsidizing hardware in exchange for data-driven lending rights**. In other words, the hardware is not priced to recover its full cost up front; it is priced to secure payment flow, data access, and adjacent financial-services revenue.

Pix reinforces this. Mordor says **Pix by Proximity** launched in **February 2025**, extending tap-style interaction to instant payments. Castles states the central bank launched **PIX Automático** and **PIX por Aproximação** in **June 2025**, expanding usage into recurring and tap-to-pay contexts. The source counts differ—Mordor cites **42 billion Pix transactions in 2024**, while Castles cites **57 billion**—so the exact total needs current verification, but both support the same conclusion: Pix scale is large enough to shape device design and merchant economics.

### Typical price ranges

Brazil provides the clearest official-store evidence in the report:
- **Mercado Pago Point Smart 2**: about **12x R$16.65** or similar promotional variants
- **PagBank Moderninha Smart 2**: **12x R$16.34** or **R$196.08 cash**
- **Ton T3**: **R$108 cash** or **12x R$9**

These are strikingly low compared with North American list prices because they are **merchant-acquisition prices**, not clean device ASPs.

### Installed-base proxies

Brazil offers unusually strong merchant-scale signals:
- **Stone**: **4.8 million active payment clients** in the 2025 IR snapshot; company highlights note **4.0 million clients** reached in 2024
- **PagBank**: **6.3 million active merchants** in Q4 2024
- **Pix**: **16 million businesses** using it after roughly 3.5 years according to Matera

These figures are not additive because of merchant overlap and because many merchants accept payments through QR or tap-on-phone without a tablet-style device.

### Estimated installed base

**Estimate (active tablet-style devices, South America):**
- **Low:** 2.8 million
- **Base:** 4.5 million
- **High:** 6.5 million
- **Confidence:** **Medium-low**

**Why this range is defensible.** Brazil dominates the regional base, and the **63.72% mobile/portable shipment share** indicates the center of gravity is already in portable smart hardware rather than fixed legacy estates. But the **4.8 million Stone active clients**, **6.3 million PagBank merchants**, and **16 million Pix-accepting businesses** cannot be read as hardware counts because overlap is substantial and many acceptance paths do not require a tablet-like device. The 4.5 million base estimate therefore assumes millions of subsidized smart/mobile POS devices in Brazil plus a smaller but meaningful base across Argentina, Chile, Colombia, Peru, and other markets.

---

## Cross-Regional Comparison and Sizing Synthesis

### Regional comparison table

| Region | Leading vendors / models | Typical deployment scenarios | Channel structure | Indicative price range | Penetration evidence | Installed-base estimate | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **North America** | Clover Flex/Mini/Station, Square Handheld/Register/Stand, Toast Go/Flex, Shopify POS Terminal, Zebra/Honeywell in enterprise | Countertop retail, restaurants, tableside, kiosk, omnichannel, enterprise field tablets | Direct SMB brands + software-led sales + bank/acquirer channels | ~$149–$899 list for mainstream Square hardware; Shopify POS Terminal $349; Clover channel pricing ~$749–$2,099 | Clover 4M+ devices shipped; Square 4M+ sellers; Toast ~164k locations; North America largest mobile POS region in summaries | **6.5m / 9.0m / 12.5m** | Medium |
| **Japan** | PayCAS Mobile / PAX A920-class, QR-led acceptance, app-capable smart terminals | Tourism, food trucks, restaurants, QR-rich SMB, transit-linked retail, healthcare/admin | Acquirer/telco/service bundles | Public local pricing weak; global PAX-class proxy ~$375–$393 | 42.8% cashless ratio in 2024; 3m+ wallet-accepting terminals; 75.8m QR users in 2023 |  | Medium-low |
| **Korea** | Domestic VAN/device ecosystem + Android POS + mobile devices | Chain retail, convenience, delivery, in-aisle, hospitality, healthcare | VAN/dealer-installed | KRW 200,000 NFC-compliant upgrade anchor; advanced smartPOS pricing opaque | 2.9m merchant locations; 2m+ Zero Pay businesses; only ~10% NFC-compliant readers by Mar 2025 | **Japan+Korea combined: 1.8m / 2.8m / 4.0m** | Medium-low |
| **Southeast Asia** | PAX, SUNMI, local SME POS apps, Android smartPOS, QR/NFC hybrids | Kiosks, pop-ups, food trucks, mobile merchants, low-cost retail, wallet/QR acceptance | Fragmented acquirer + SaaS + distributor channels | Device-only proxy ~$375–$479; fuller solutions $1,500–$3,000 + fees in one summary | 46.25% mobile/portable revenue share; 56.97% contactless revenue share; DealPOS+Qasir 500k merchants | **2.2m / 3.8m / 5.8m** | Low-medium |
| **South America** | Brazil-centric fintech smart terminals: Point Smart 2, Moderninha Smart 2, Ton; PAX/Ingenico/Verifone OEM presence | SMB checkout, logistics, restaurants, services, healthcare, transport | Fintech/acquirer subsidies + reseller channels | Brazil promo pricing roughly R$108–R$207 for mainstream smart terminals | Brazil 68% contactless terminal penetration; 4.8m Stone active payment clients; 6.3m PagBank active merchants | **2.8m / 4.5m / 6.5m** | Medium-low |

### Cross-regional synthesis

- **North America** is strongest where merchants intentionally buy a full commerce stack—hardware, software, and payments from one brand.
- **Japan and Korea** are stronger examples of **channel-shaped adoption**. Local rails, telco/acquirer packaging, and dealer-led installation influence which device class wins more than visible open-market MSRP does.
- **Southeast Asia** has the weakest one-to-one link between payment digitization and dedicated hardware because QR and wallets can digitize checkout without a full tablet POS purchase.
- **South America**, especially Brazil, has the strongest case where **ultra-low upfront prices** do not mean low-end hardware; they mean the hardware is financed by downstream payment and lending economics.

---

## Pricing Analysis and Configuration Economics

### Pricing table

| Model / family | Region | Observed price | Bundle status | Notes |

| Square Stand | North America | $149 | Hardware only; iPad required | Tablet not included |
| Square Terminal | North America | $299 | Integrated payment device | Built-in printer |
| Square Handheld | North America | $399 | Integrated handheld | Tableside and barcode-scan use |

| Shopify POS Terminal | North America | $349 | Unbundled terminal | Pairs with tablet/phone |
| Shopify POS Terminal Countertop Kit | North America | $459 | Bundled with stand; tablet excluded | Countertop solution |
| Clover Flex | North America | ~$749 | Channel / reseller | Indicative, not official MSRP |
| Clover Mini | North America | ~$949 | Channel / reseller | Indicative, not official MSRP |
| Clover Station Solo | North America | ~$1,799 | Channel / reseller | Indicative, not official MSRP |
| Clover Station Duo | North America | ~$2,099 | Channel / reseller | Indicative, not official MSRP |
| Toast Starter Kit | North America | $0 upfront on some plans | Processing-tied bundle | Hardware economics embedded in processing |
| PAX A920MAX | Global open market proxy | ~$455 | Device-only | SmartPOS comparator |
